sections.text: Welcome back. AP Computer Science A, Module Two — using objects. Last module we covered primitive types: ints, doubles, booleans. Today we step up to objects, which is where Java really comes alive.

Think about your phone. Your phone is an object. It HAS data — battery percentage, contact list, photos. And it DOES things — it can ring, send texts, take pictures. Java objects work exactly the same way. They bundle data and behavior together. Once you see this pattern, the rest of Java falls into place.

Three things to lock in today. One — the difference between primitive types and reference types, and why it matters. Two — the String class and the Math class, both of which you will use in literally every program from here on. Three — how to create objects with the new keyword, and the equals versus equals-equals trap that catches almost every beginner.

First, the core distinction. A primitive variable stores the actual value directly in the variable. An int is just a number sitting there. A reference variable stores an address that points to an object somewhere in memory. String, Random, Scanner — all of these are reference types. The variable does not hold the object itself. It holds directions to the object.

Strings are the most common reference type you will use. Here are the four methods that show up constantly. Length gives you the number of characters. Substring gives you a slice. IndexOf finds a character or returns negative one if it's missing. ToUpperCase returns a brand new uppercase version. Memorize these four — they appear on every AP exam.

Critical fact about Strings. They are immutable, meaning you can never change a String once it exists. When you write s equals s plus exclamation mark, Java does not edit the original. It creates a brand new String and points your variable at it. The original is still floating in memory, unchanged. This will matter later for performance.

Next, the Math class. Math is a utility class built into Java. You never create a Math object — you just call its methods directly. Math dot abs gives you absolute value. Math dot pow raises a number to a power. Math dot sqrt is square root. And Math dot random gives you a random double between zero and one, super useful for games and simulations.

Quick check. Compute Math dot pow of two and eight — what value do you get? And what does the substring of zero comma three return when called on the String Genesis? Hint — Math dot pow returns a double, not an int. And substring uses inclusive comma exclusive indexing. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Time. Math dot pow of two and eight is two hundred fifty six point zero. Notice the point zero — that's because pow always returns a double. And Genesis dot substring of zero comma three returns the String G-e-n. The starting index is included, the ending index is not. So you get characters at positions zero, one, and two.

One more concept — wrapper classes. Sometimes you need to treat a primitive like an object. The Integer class wraps an int, the Double class wraps a double. Why do you care? Because collections like ArrayList can only hold objects, not primitives. Java will auto-box your int into an Integer when needed, but you should know the wrapper exists.

Creating an object uses the new keyword. The pattern is always the same — type, variable name, equals, new, type, parentheses. For a Random number generator, you write Random r equals new Random. For Strings you usually skip new and just write a literal in quotes, but the long form works too. Learn the pattern, you'll use it everywhere.

Here's the trap that costs students points every single exam. When you compare Strings with equals-equals, you are comparing memory addresses, not contents. That is almost never what you want. The correct way is to call dot equals as a method. The two might LOOK the same with String literals, but the moment one is created with new, equals-equals breaks. Always use dot equals for Strings.

Quick recap. Primitive holds a value, reference holds an address. String gives you length, substring, indexOf, and toUpperCase. Math gives you random, abs, pow, and sqrt. Use new ClassName with parentheses to create objects. And critically — always use dot equals, never equals-equals, when comparing Strings. Five rules. Lock them in.

Reminder — this video is the overview. To master objects, here's the rest. Read the Using Objects chapter on Runestone. Knock out the String-One problem set on Codingbat — that's ten short problems and they really cement the String methods. Do the assignment in your dashboard. And book an advisor check-in if equals-equals versus dot equals still feels fuzzy. Next up, Module Three — boolean expressions and if statements.
